method: Fresh verifier sub-agent (direct fetch of the primary Executive Order text on governor.ny.gov, plus web searches for independent corroboration) followed by an independent adversarial falsifier sub-agent (re-checked the primary text directly, checked for conflation with unrelated local/municipal data-center actions, checked for industry/political response, checked the 'first state' superlative).
origin_items: Surfaced via this cycle's fresh ingest (gnews-ai-regulation-and-policy.json: multiple outlets covering the executive order and Trump's reaction on 2026-07-17). dedup-check run before opening: no match.

claims.text: On 14 July 2026, Governor Kathy Hochul signed Executive Order 62, directing the state Department of Environmental Conservation to hold in abeyance new discretionary permit applications for data centers of 50 megawatts or more, while the Department of Public Service prepares a Generic Environmental Impact Statement on the sector
claims.reasoning: Confirmed directly against the primary EO 62 text on governor.ny.gov (fetched by both the verifier and, independently, the falsifier), and cross-checked against Axios and CBS New York, both independently bylined and not wire reprints of each other.

claims.text: The order explicitly cites demand from artificial intelligence, cloud computing, and streaming services, and references roughly 12 gigawatts of pending grid interconnection requests, as its rationale
claims.reasoning: Direct quote/paraphrase from the EO's own text, confirmed by both sub-agents on direct fetch -- not a press paraphrase.

claims.text: The order sets no fixed expiration date, tying its end to completion of the state's environmental review; officials and press have described the expected timeline as up to one year
claims.reasoning: The EO text itself ties duration to GEIS completion with no calendar end date; the 'up to one year' figure is the governor's office's own press characterization (confirmed via the governor.ny.gov press release), not a hard clause in the order -- kept distinct in the article's phrasing per the falsifier's instruction.

claims.text: President Trump criticized the order on Truth Social, calling it a 'terrible decision' and urging New York to change the policy 'immediately,' saying Hochul acted 'for political reasons'
claims.reasoning: Corroborated by multiple independently-bylined outlets (CNBC, Baltimore Sun, Seeking Alpha, Benzinga, Mid Hudson News) all quoting the same Truth Social post consistently.

claims.text: Hochul publicly defended the order in response to Trump's criticism
claims.reasoning: Confirmed via WXXI News' independent coverage of the exchange; kept the article's own text to a general characterization rather than quoting a specific line neither sub-agent independently re-verified against a primary transcript within the cycle's time budget.

claims.text: The Data Center Coalition, an industry group, said the moratorium would push investment, jobs, and tax revenue to other states, citing 2024 figures of over 227,000 New York jobs and $49 billion in state GDP tied to the sector
claims.reasoning: Directly attributed statement from the Data Center Coalition's EVP of state policy, reported by The Hill; added per the falsifier's explicit instruction to include industry/advocacy reaction for balance, since the initial draft only carried Trump's political reaction.

falsifier_notes: Overall verdict: PUBLISH. Falsifier independently re-fetched the primary EO text and confirmed every core fact (date, 50MW threshold, abeyance mechanism, AI/12GW rationale, open-ended duration) verbatim against the order's own language -- no discrepancies with the verifier's report. Checked for conflation with same-week local/municipal data-center actions reported in the same news cycle (Brookhaven Town's own separate 18-month local moratorium tied to a specific Yaphank project, plus unrelated Fort Worth TX and Oshkosh WI municipal zoning votes) and confirmed these are independent local actions, not part of or caused by EO 62 -- the article does not reference them, avoiding the conflation risk entirely rather than needing an explicit disambiguation sentence. Confirmed the 'first statewide moratorium' superlative is independently corroborated by CNBC, NBC News, CBS News, and the governor's own release, with no outlet disputing it; article attributes it via multiple named sources rather than asserting it as an unattributed house fact. Flagged that the initial claim set was missing industry/advocacy reaction beyond Trump's political criticism -- added the Data Center Coalition's on-the-record statement (The Hill) for balance before publish. No editorializing found in either language draft; both direct quotes (Trump, Data Center Coalition) are accurately attributed, not stated as fact.
